name: Cloud SQL High Availability and Replication
description: Configuring Cloud SQL high availability with standby instances, read replicas, cross-region replicas, and understanding failover behavior.
key_facts: Cloud SQL HA uses a regional persistent disk that replicates synchronously to a standby instance in a different zone
Automatic failover to standby takes 1-2 minutes; DNS is updated to point to the new primary automatically
Read replicas (same region, cross-region, or external) offload read traffic; cross-region replicas enable regional DR
Cascaded replicas: a read replica can have its own replica, enabling read distribution across multiple tiers
Point-in-time recovery (PITR) requires binary logging (MySQL) or WAL archiving (PostgreSQL) stored in Cloud Storage
common_misconceptions: HA standby instances serve read traffic — the standby is in hot standby mode and does not serve queries; use read replicas for scaling reads
Cross-region replica provides zero data loss — asynchronous replication means some data loss (RPO > 0) is possible during regional failover

name: Cloud Spanner Global Scale and Consistency
description: Designing schemas and applications for Cloud Spanner with proper key design to avoid hotspots, interleaved tables, and multi-region configurations.
key_facts: Spanner provides external consistency (strongest isolation level) using TrueTime API (GPS + atomic clocks)
Row key design: avoid monotonically increasing keys (causes write hotspots); use UUID, bit-reversed sequences, or hashed keys
Interleaved tables physically co-locate child rows with their parent row, eliminating joins for parent-child queries
Read-write transactions vs read-only transactions: read-only transactions use a snapshot and don't hold locks
Spanner Change Streams provide near-real-time CDC for downstream consumers via Dataflow or direct API polling
common_misconceptions: Spanner's global consistency means all reads see the latest data — stale reads (bounded staleness) trade latency for throughput and are common for analytics
Spanner auto-scales without any key design consideration — poor key design causes split hotspots that limit throughput regardless of node count

name: Bigtable Schema Design and Operations
description: Designing Bigtable schemas with optimal row key structures for time-series, IoT, and operational analytics use cases at scale.
key_facts: Wide-column model: row key (sorted lexicographically), column family, column qualifier, timestamp, cell value
Time-series row key pattern: reverse timestamp suffix prevents hotspots (e.g., device#9999999999-timestamp)
Column families have independent garbage collection: max versions and max age per column family
Bigtable replication (multi-cluster routing) enables failover and geo-distributed low-latency reads
cbt CLI and Key Visualizer are essential tools for inspecting data distribution and diagnosing hotspots
common_misconceptions: Bigtable rows can have arbitrary numbers of column families — column families are defined at table creation and adding too many degrades performance
Bigtable automatically indexes all columns — only the row key is indexed; design row keys to support all query patterns

name: Firestore Document Database Design
description: Designing Firestore data models for web and mobile applications using document-collection hierarchy, composite indexes, and security rules.
key_facts: Firestore document size limit is 1 MB; avoid storing large BLOBs in documents; use Cloud Storage for files
Composite indexes are required for queries combining multiple inequality filters or ordering by a non-equality field
Subcollection vs root collection tradeoff: subcollections co-locate related data but make cross-document queries harder
Firestore security rules: auth-based rules prevent unauthorized access; field validation rules enforce data integrity
Firestore supports up to 1 write per second per document; use distributed counters for high-frequency counting
common_misconceptions: Firestore automatically indexes all fields — array and map field members require explicit index configuration
Transactions in Firestore lock entire collections — transactions only lock the specific documents they read or write

name: Memorystore for Redis and Memcached
description: Selecting and configuring Memorystore for Redis or Memcached as caching, session storage, and message broker layers in cloud applications.
key_facts: Memorystore for Redis Standard Tier: replicated instance across two zones with automatic failover
Redis Cluster (Sharded Redis) in Memorystore supports horizontal scaling across multiple shards
Redis data types: Strings, Lists, Sets, Sorted Sets, Hashes, Streams; each suited to different caching patterns
Memorystore is VPC-only; requires Serverless VPC Access for Cloud Run or App Engine connections
RDB snapshots and AOF persistence options in Memorystore Standard; Basic Tier has no persistence
common_misconceptions: Memorystore Standard Tier eliminates data loss on failure — replication reduces loss risk, but some in-flight writes may be lost during failover
Redis Cluster and Redis replication (HA) are the same — HA provides failover of one instance; Cluster shards data across multiple nodes for scale

name: Database Migration Strategies and Tools
description: Planning and executing database migrations to GCP using Database Migration Service (DMS) and Datastream for homogeneous and heterogeneous migrations.
key_facts: Database Migration Service supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server, Oracle to Cloud SQL, AlloyDB, and Spanner
DMS uses logical replication (CDC) enabling minimal downtime migrations with continuous change capture
Connection profile defines source and destination connection parameters; migration job orchestrates the transfer
Datastream provides CDC-based replication from Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL to BigQuery, Cloud Storage, and Cloud SQL
Heterogeneous migrations (e.g., Oracle to PostgreSQL) require schema conversion using Database Migration Service's conversion workspace
common_misconceptions: DMS automatically converts schema between database engines — heterogeneous migrations require schema review and manual SQL conversion for stored procedures and triggers
Minimal downtime migrations require zero downtime — a brief cutover window is still required to sync final changes and switch connection strings

name: AlloyDB for PostgreSQL High Performance
description: Using AlloyDB as a fully managed PostgreSQL-compatible database with columnar storage engine, intelligent caching, and read pool instances for analytics.
key_facts: AlloyDB is fully compatible with PostgreSQL 14+; existing applications migrate with minimal or no code changes
AlloyDB columnar engine automatically accelerates analytical queries by up to 100x over standard PostgreSQL
Read pool instances are automatically scalable read replicas; traffic auto-distributes across the pool
AlloyDB Omni is the downloadable version of AlloyDB that runs anywhere (on-premises, other clouds)
AI-assisted indexing and query insights automatically recommend missing indexes and explain plan improvements
common_misconceptions: AlloyDB replaces Cloud SQL for all PostgreSQL workloads — AlloyDB is optimized for high-throughput OLTP and HTAP; Cloud SQL remains simpler and cheaper for smaller workloads
AlloyDB columnar engine requires manual configuration — the columnar engine is fully automatic; no DDL changes needed

name: Database Security and Access Control
description: Implementing database security with IAM database authentication, CMEK, network security, and audit logging for GCP managed databases.
key_facts: Cloud SQL IAM authentication allows users and service accounts to authenticate with IAM credentials instead of DB passwords
CMEK encrypts database data with customer-managed keys in Cloud KMS for data sovereignty
Private IP (VPC peering) is preferred over public IP for Cloud SQL; authorized networks restrict public IP access by CIDR
Cloud SQL Audit Logs capture data access events when enabled; Spanner Data Access Logs track all reads/writes
Database flags (e.g., log_connections, log_min_duration_statement in PostgreSQL) enhance security logging
common_misconceptions: IAM database authentication replaces database roles entirely — IAM controls who can connect; database roles and privileges still control what they can do once connected
Private IP in Cloud SQL eliminates all attack surface — the database is still accessible from any VM in the peered VPC; use firewall rules and IAM

name: Database Selection Decision Framework
description: Selecting the right GCP database service based on consistency requirements, scale, query patterns, latency, and operational needs.
key_facts: Decision matrix: OLTP + global scale + strong consistency -> Spanner; OLTP + regional + SQL -> Cloud SQL/AlloyDB
High-throughput NoSQL (IoT, time-series, analytics serving) -> Bigtable; document model with real-time sync -> Firestore
OLAP / analytics -> BigQuery; in-memory caching/session -> Memorystore (Redis)
AlloyDB for PostgreSQL-compatible HTAP (hybrid transactional/analytical) workloads needing both OLTP and analytics
Data volume tipping points: Cloud SQL up to ~10 TB; Spanner scales to petabytes; Bigtable scales to exabytes
common_misconceptions: There is one right database for all workloads — modern applications often use multiple database services (polyglot persistence)
The cheapest database is always best — choosing the wrong database often results in 10x more application complexity

name: Database Disaster Recovery and Backup Strategies
description: Designing database disaster recovery strategies with backup policies, cross-region replication, and failover procedures to meet RPO/RTO requirements.
key_facts: Cloud SQL automated backups (daily) and on-demand backups stored in Cloud Storage; retained for up to 365 days
Cross-region read replicas for Cloud SQL provide a warm standby for regional DR with manual failover promotion
Cloud Spanner multi-region configurations provide 99.999% SLA with automatic failover across regions
Bigtable cluster replication with failover routing policy enables automatic regional failover
RTO and RPO requirements drive database tier selection: Spanner multi-region (RPO=0, RTO minutes); Cloud SQL cross-region replica (RPO minutes, RTO hours)
common_misconceptions: Cloud SQL backups are in the same region as the database — you can configure backups to use a different region for DR
Manual failover of a Cloud SQL replica is seamless — promoting a read replica requires DNS updates and application reconnection

name: Database Performance Tuning and Monitoring
description: Monitoring and optimizing database performance using Cloud SQL Insights, Spanner Query Insights, and database-specific optimization techniques.
key_facts: Cloud SQL Insights provides query performance analysis, slow query identification, and wait events for MySQL and PostgreSQL
Spanner Query Insights visualizes query execution plans, CPU usage, and latency percentiles for optimization
Index tuning: EXPLAIN ANALYZE in Cloud SQL; Query Plans in Spanner; Key Visualizer for Bigtable key distribution
Connection pooling (PgBouncer, HikariCP) is critical for Cloud SQL to prevent connection exhaustion from serverless workloads
Cloud SQL flag pg_stat_statements (PostgreSQL) and slow_query_log (MySQL) provide workload-level query analytics
common_misconceptions: Adding more indexes always improves performance — over-indexing slows write operations and increases storage; index only frequently-queried columns
Increasing machine size always solves performance issues — identify the bottleneck first (CPU, I/O, network) before scaling vertically

name: BigQuery for Analytical Database Workloads
description: Using BigQuery as the analytical layer in a multi-database architecture with federated queries, connected sheets, and INFORMATION_SCHEMA for operational intelligence.
key_facts: BigQuery Omni queries data in AWS S3 and Azure Blob Storage without moving data, using BigQuery SQL
Federated queries in BigQuery can query Cloud SQL, Cloud Storage (CSV, Parquet), Bigtable, and Google Sheets
BigQuery external tables allow querying Cloud Storage data without loading; loading is faster for repeated queries
Analytics Hub enables sharing BigQuery datasets across organizations as data exchanges
BigQuery BI Engine provides in-memory analytics acceleration for Looker Studio and Google Sheets connections
common_misconceptions: Federated queries are as fast as native BigQuery tables — federated queries have higher latency due to network round-trips to source systems
BigQuery replaces operational databases — BigQuery is an analytics warehouse; operational databases handle OLTP with low-latency transactions

name: Schema Migration and Version Management
description: Managing database schema changes with online DDL (Cloud Spanner, AlloyDB), migration tools, and zero-downtime schema migration patterns.
key_facts: Cloud Spanner supports online schema changes that do not block reads or writes; changes are applied in the background
AlloyDB and Cloud SQL support pg_repack and fast DDL for minimal lock duration schema changes
Schema migration tools: Liquibase, Flyway, Alembic integrate with Cloud Build for automated migration pipelines
Backward-compatible migrations: add nullable columns, new tables; avoid removing or renaming columns in running applications
Expand-contract pattern: expand schema (add new structure), migrate data, update code, contract (remove old structure)
common_misconceptions: Spanner schema changes are instantaneous — they are online but have a propagation time across all Spanner nodes
DROP COLUMN in PostgreSQL is always fast — PostgreSQL marks the column as dropped logically but physical removal requires VACUUM FULL

name: Database Migration Assessment and Planning
description: Assessing current database workloads, identifying migration complexity, and selecting the optimal GCP target database using migration readiness tools.
key_facts: Database Migration Service assessment profiles source database and identifies compatibility issues before migration
Cloud SQL migration from AWS RDS is supported via DMS using logical replication without downtime
Oracle to PostgreSQL migration: use pgloader or ora2pg for schema and data conversion; DMS for ongoing replication
INFORMATION_SCHEMA queries on source database provide table sizes, index usage, and stored procedure inventory for assessment
Cloud Architecture Diagramming tools help document the current state and target state for stakeholder communication
common_misconceptions: Database migration is primarily a technical exercise — business continuity planning and stakeholder change management are equally critical
All Oracle features are available in PostgreSQL — Oracle has proprietary features (CONNECT BY, ROWNUM, packages) requiring rewriting in PostgreSQL

name: Datastream Change Data Capture for Real-Time Replication
description: Using Datastream to continuously replicate database changes from Oracle, MySQL, and PostgreSQL sources to BigQuery and Cloud Storage for real-time analytics and migration.
key_facts: Datastream uses CDC (Change Data Capture) to stream row-level changes from source databases in near real-time
Supported sources: Oracle (LogMiner), MySQL (binary log), PostgreSQL (logical replication); destinations: BigQuery, Cloud Storage
Private connectivity options: IP allowlisting, forward-SSH tunnel, or VPC peering for secure source connectivity
Datastream to BigQuery automatically creates and updates BigQuery tables to match source schema changes
Datastream integrates with Dataflow templates for transformations before landing data in the destination
common_misconceptions: Datastream replaces Database Migration Service — Datastream is for ongoing replication and analytics; DMS is for one-time migrations with cutover
Datastream guarantees exactly-once delivery to BigQuery — it provides at-least-once semantics; BigQuery deduplication handles duplicates via unique keys
